backfill_note: The measurements in results.json are VERBATIM from the session's firsthand notes taken on 2026-07-05 (used_memory bytes, key counts, rps figures). No harness script existed at the time: redis-benchmark was invoked ad hoc from the shell, and the invocations were recorded in prose. run.sh here is a RECONSTRUCTION of those recorded invocations (same flags: -n/-r/-d/-t/-P/-c), authored 2026-07-23 and syntax-checked, not the literal file executed on 2026-07-05. Two details of the original run are NOT recorded and are therefore chosen by run.sh rather than recovered: (a) which redis-benchmark client binary was used against each server, and (b) whether each phase started from a freshly booted server. See results.json -> explicitly_not_verified.

environment.topology: single host, both servers as local containers, client on the same host
environment.note: Single-host microbenchmark. Not a production topology, no repeated-run p99 distribution. Absolute numbers are machine-bound; the durable signal is the DIRECTION of each comparison (memory consistently favors Valkey; throughput flips with pipeline depth).

subject.name: Valkey 9.1
subject.version_note: INFO reports redis_version:7.2.4 (RESP-compat field) alongside valkey_version:9.1.0 — the 7.2.4 string is a compatibility marker, not the actual server generation.

pins.policy: Both engines are pinned BY DIGEST, not by tag. The tags valkey/valkey:9.1 and redis:8 are moving targets and will point at newer builds over time; the digests above are what was measured. Both digests were re-verified as resolvable on Docker Hub on 2026-07-23 (docker manifest inspect).
pins.client_binary: run.sh drives BOTH servers with the redis-benchmark shipped inside the redis:8 image (pinned by the same digest), so the client is held constant across engines. The original 2026-07-05 run did not record which client binary hit which server.

workload.memory_phase: redis-benchmark -n 1000000 -r 1000000 -d 64 -t set -P 32 (random keyspace of 1e6, 64-byte values), then INFO memory / DBSIZE
workload.note: The memory phase inserts ~632.5k distinct keys out of a 1e6 random keyspace (collisions are expected and are why the count is not 1e6); the two engines landed within +/-100 keys of each other, which is what makes the memory comparison apples-to-apples.

determinism.note: Key counts land near 632.5k but are not bit-stable across runs (random keyspace). used_memory tracks the key count closely, so compare the RATIO between engines at similar DBSIZE rather than the absolute MB. Throughput rps varies run-to-run; the unpipelined run was repeated once per engine (both repeats recorded in results.json) while the pipelined run is single-shot (n=1) — treat its ~10% delta as the weakest claim in this run.

reverification.what: run.sh was executed in SMOKE=1 mode (1/100 scale: 10k inserts over a 10k keyspace) twice on the same Windows/WSL2 host, to confirm the reconstructed harness actually runs and that the memory direction still holds on the pinned digests with a single constant client.
reverification.outcome: Server versions confirmed (valkey_version 9.1.0 / redis_version 8.8.0, both jemalloc-5.3.0). Memory per key: Valkey 279.7 B and 277.1 B across the two smoke runs, Redis 373.6 B and 372.7 B — Valkey 25.1% and 25.7% lower, consistent with the 23.2% recorded at full scale.
reverification.limit: SMOKE mode does NOT resolve the throughput comparison: at 5k-10k requests redis-benchmark's rps output is coarse and both engines landed in the same band. Only the full-scale run speaks to throughput.
reverification.artifact: smoke-2026-07-23.txt (verbatim stdout of the second smoke run)

raw_artifacts.retention_policy: Containers were started with --rm and named firsthand-valkey-91-redis-alternative-*; both pulled images were removed after the run per the workspace cleanup rule.
raw_artifacts.note: No raw redis-benchmark stdout was retained — results.json carries the transcribed summary figures only.
